# Methods for Communicating Uncertainty {#sec-methods}

The eight methods reviewed in this section represent the principal approaches
through which uncertain information is represented and conveyed to audiences
across disciplines. They range from fully visual methods — forecast
trajectories, fan charts, and distributional displays — through numerical
summaries — prediction intervals and quantiles — to categorical systems,
narrative approaches, and linguistic and numerical expressions of probability.
No single method is universally superior; each has particular strengths,
characteristic limitations, and audiences for which it is best suited. The
review of each method follows a consistent structure: an opening description
of what the method is and how it works; a practical example with a figure
placeholder indicating a specific source figure to reproduce or recreate; an
assessment of strengths and limitations, including where the literature is
divided; guidance on when the method is most appropriate; and a set of
evidence-based good practice recommendations.

A cross-cutting theme across all eight methods is the importance of calibration
— the property that stated probabilities correspond to observed frequencies.
A method that is technically sophisticated but poorly calibrated provides a
false picture of uncertainty and may harm decision-making more than a simpler
but well-calibrated alternative. Calibration should be verified and reported
wherever possible, regardless of the method chosen.

A second cross-cutting theme is the tension between completeness and
cognitive load. More informative displays — full distributions, ensemble
trajectories, multi-band fan charts — communicate more about the structure
of uncertainty but impose greater demands on the audience. Simpler displays —
point estimates, categorical labels, verbal expressions — reduce cognitive
burden but at the cost of information. Matching the level of complexity to
the audience and the decision context, as discussed in @sec-why, is the
central design challenge across all methods.
